coredata.dc:title: Hydrogen bonding donor/acceptor active sites exposed on imide-functionalized carbon dots aid in enhancing arsenic adsorption performance
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Ze
Jin, Limin
Liu, Yanbiao
Wang, Ying
coredata.dc:description: During the arsenic (As) adsorption process, amino-functionalization has been widely considered as one of the effective adsorbents optimization methods, while this study reveals that imide functional groups also play a non-negligible role. Herein, magnetic carbon dots (CDs) equipped with imide (FeCD-NH) or amino functional groups (FeCD-NH2) were synthesized to deduce the relationship between different configurations of amino-like functional groups and As. The results showed that FeCD-NH exhibited extraordinary As(V) adsorption behavior (with 97.80 % As(V) removal within 5 min), outperformed that equipped with amino functional groups (FeCD-NH2). An array of advanced characterization techniques and density functional theory (DFT) calculation collectively revealed a dual-configuration hydrogen bond synergistic mechanism of FeCD-NH toward As(V). Different from monomorphic hydrogen bond of amino functional group, imide functional group forms abundant adsorption sites through dual-configuration hydrogen bonds to realize the ultrafast adsorption of As(V). In addition, FeCD-NH can remove As(V) from other more complex water matrices to levels below the World Health Organization (WHO) drinking water standards and the Chinese surface water standards. Therefore, this work provides a promising strategy for As decontamination by fine-tune the surface chemistry of adsorbents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Arsenic
Imide-functionalized
Carbon dots
Hydrogen-bond
Selective removal

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Adsorption performance of –NH and –NH2 functional groups for As(V)
Mechanism of as adsorption on FeCD-NH and FeCD-NH2
Adsorption isotherm model and adsorption kinetics
Mechanism of As(V) adsorption on FeCD-NH and FeCD-NH2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of FeCD-NH preparation (a); TEM (b) and HR-TEM image of FeCD-NH (c); XRD patterns (d); FTIR spectra of maleimide, MCD and FeCD-NH (e).

Adsorption of As(V) by FeCD, FeCD-NH2 and FeCD-NH (a); removal efficiency and adsorption rate constant of the pseudo-second-order kinetics of As(V) by FeCD-NH2 and FeCD-NH (b); removal efficiency and adsorption rate constant of the pseudo-second-order kinetics of As(V) adsorption by FeCD-NH synthesized at different ratios.
Ph variation of fecd-nh and fecd-nh2 in As(V) solution (a); variation in As(V) speciation at different pH (b); As(V) adsorption by FeCD-NH (c) and FeCD-NH2 (d) at different pH.

FTIR spectra of FeCD-NH (a) and FeCD-NH2 (b) before and after As(V) adsorption; N 1 s XPS spectra of FeCD-NH (c) and FeCD-NH2 (d) before and after As(V) adsorption. C 1 s XPS spectra of FeCD-NH (e) and FeCD-NH2 (f) before and after As(V) adsorption.

Optimal configurations obtained via DFT calculations of H-bonding between FeCD-NH (a), FeCD-NH2 (b) and As(V) (gray for C, white for H, blue for N, red for O and purple for As). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Different adsorption mechanisms of As(V) on FeCD-NH2 and FeCD-NH.
Cyclic adsorption performance of As(V) by FeCD-NH (a); adsorption of As(V) by FeCD-NH in simulated natural water.

head.ce:title: Hydrogen bonding donor/acceptor active sites exposed on imide-functionalized carbon dots aid in enhancing arsenic adsorption performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resources Reuse, College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Tongji University, Shanghai 200092, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resources Reuse
College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resources Reuse, College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Tongji University, Shanghai 200092, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Institute of Pollution Control and Ecological Security, Shanghai 200092, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Institute of Pollution Control and Ecological Security

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Institute of Pollution Control and Ecological Security, Shanghai 200092, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: During the arsenic (As) adsorption process, amino-functionalization has been widely considered as one of the effective adsorbents optimization methods, while this study reveals that imide functional groups also play a non-negligible role. Herein, magnetic carbon dots (CDs) equipped with imide (FeCD-NH) or amino functional groups (FeCD-NH2) were synthesized to deduce the relationship between different configurations of amino-like functional groups and As. The results showed that FeCD-NH exhibited extraordinary As(V) adsorption behavior (with 97.80 % As(V) removal within 5 min), outperformed that equipped with amino functional groups (FeCD-NH2). An array of advanced characterization techniques and density functional theory (DFT) calculation collectively revealed a dual-configuration hydrogen bond synergistic mechanism of FeCD-NH toward As(V). Different from monomorphic hydrogen bond of amino functional group, imide functional group forms abundant adsorption sites through dual-configuration hydrogen bonds to realize the ultrafast adsorption of As(V). In addition, FeCD-NH can remove As(V) from other more complex water matrices to levels below the World Health Organization (WHO) drinking water standards and the Chinese surface water standards. Therefore, this work provides a promising strategy for As decontamination by fine-tune the surface chemistry of adsorbents.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Arsenic (As) contamination in groundwater affects millions of people worldwide due to its high toxicity, easy accumulation and non-biodegradable [1–6]. Consequently, As pollution treatment is an important issue worldwide, and an efficient As removal solution is urgently needed. There are three main states of As exist in water, arsenite (H3AsO3, As(III)), arsenate (H3AsO4, As(V)) and arsine. Although these organic- and inorganic-arsenic exist in different chemical structures, they all have similar HxAsOy
n
− configuration [7–9]. Hence, the crux of seeking As treatment methods is to focus on the establishment of an interface that can effectively bind with HxAsOy
n
−.

To date, numerous efforts have been devoted to establishing a strong interaction interface with As, including adsorption [10,11], membrane filtration [12,13], electrocoagulation [14,15], which have been confirmed that include electrostatic attraction, hydrophobic, and van der Waals force as the main interfacial interaction mechanisms [16–19]. For example, Rahman et al. synthesized a biochar metal oxide composite and confirmed that electrostatic attraction is one of the main adsorption mechanisms for its efficient As(V) adsorption [9]. It is important to note that interference by coexisting species, such as anions and heavy metal ions, greatly reduces the removal efficiency of target contaminants in complex environmental media. Consequently, more specific interaction sites need to be designed according to the special structure of contaminants to achieve efficient and targeted treatment. Hydrogen bonding is a highly selective interaction, which has been confirmed as one of the essential mechanisms during contaminants removal according to recent studies [20,21]. Attribute to the Aδ−-Hδ+···Dδ− electrostatic attraction, hydrogen bond is caused by the polarization of the A-H bond and the presentation of protons approached by the lone pair of electrons of the D atom [22,23]. This special interatomic interaction endows hydrogen bonds high selectivity for oxygen anion contaminants. Since As has the structure of HxAsOy nδ−, hydrogen bonding may occur when As was adsorbed on a surface composed of functional groups, such as –OH, -CO, –NH2, etc. Therefore, delineating this hydrogen-bonding interface is critical for regulating the binding sites with As, which can serve as a targeting site for As adsorption and enhance intermolecular interactions. Besides, in-depth study on the mechanism of hydrogen bond formation can promote the establishment of a more solid As-hydrogen bond interface.

In previous studies, amino-functionalization of the adsorbent was quite effective for hydrogen bond introduction, thus enhancing the adsorption performance. Apparently, as one of the amino-liked functional groups, the unique conformation of the imide functional group (–NH) enables it to act as both proton donor and acceptor for hydrogen bonding, which is also expected to enhance the adsorption capability. However, there is still a lack of relevant studies. Besides, adsorbent dosage, duration of adsorption equilibrium, separation and regeneration of adsorbents are crucial factors concerning the cost, which is related to the industrial development and application of adsorbents [24]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a low-cost and easily separated and regenerated adsorbent for the efficient removal of As contaminants.

Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging carbon material with high specific surface area, biocompatibility and surface modification flexibility, which is conducive to its application in contaminants adsorption [25,26]. Moreover, the adjustable surface chemical property of CDs can be well used to deduce the adsorption mechanism of micro-interface functional groups. In this work, we constructed magnetic carbon dot materials with different amino functional groups (–NH and –NH2), and we expect that FeCD-NH equipped with imide functional groups is an effective and advanced adsorbent for efficient adsorption of As contaminants from water environment. Here, we aimed to (1) compare the removal efficiency and adsorption rate of FeCD-NH and FeCD-NH2 to confirm the enhanced adsorption of –NH functional groups; (2) analysis of FTIR, XPS, and DFT calculations to determine the difference between FeCD-NH and FeCD-NH2; and (3) investigate the mechanism of the imide-modification to enhance the adsorption affinity for As contaminants. This study improves our understanding of the enhanced adsorption mechanism of imide functional groups and provides useful information for designing new efficient materials for As removal.

Maleimide was purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. ltd. Ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), urea, disodium hydrogen arsenate heptahydrate (Na2HAsO4·7H2O, ≥98 %), sodium arsenite (NaAsO2), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), absolute ethanol (95 %), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), sodium phosphate (Na3PO4), sodium sulphate (Na2SO4), sodium silicate (Na2SiO3), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), copper(II) chloride dihydrate (CuCl2), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), cadmium chloride (CdCl2) and lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2) were all supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich-Fluka Chemical Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water (UP water) was used throughout all experiments. As(V) or As(III) stock solution (100 ppm) was prepared by dissolving Na2HAsO4·7H2O or NaAsO2 in UP water. All chemical reagents used in this study were analytical grade and used without further purification.

MCD: The maleimide carbon dots (MCD) were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method. Briefly, dissolving 0.25 g maleimide in 10 mL absolute ethanol and dilute to 15 mL with UP water. Then, the solution was transferred to a Teflon autoclave (20 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After reaction, the reactor was naturally cooled to room temperature. The obtained solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min and filtered by microporous membrane (0.22 μm) to separate large particles. Finally, the filtered solution was dialyzed (MW 500) against UP water for 48 h to obtain purified MCD.

FeCD-NH: In a typical synthesis, 0.231 g FeCl3·6H2O was dissolving in 1 mL absolute ethanol, and then transferred to 15 mL of the above MCD solution and 0.026 mol NaBH4 was slowed added with constant stirring. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon autoclave (20 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 14 h. The obtained black product was washed three times with absolute ethanol and UP water successively, and then freeze-dried to obtain FeCD-NH powder.

FeCD-NH2: The synthesis of FeCD-NH2 used a similar pathway to FeCD-NH. 0.231 g FeCl3·6H2O and 0.145 g urea were dissolved in 1 mL anhydrous ethanol and subsequently transferred to 15 mL CDs solution, and slowly added 0.026 mol NaBH4 under stirring condition. The black suspension was transferred to a Teflon autoclave (20 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 14 h. The obtained product was washed three times with absolute ethanol and UP water successively, and FeCD-NH2 powder was obtained after freeze-drying.

X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 ADVANCE) was carried out to characterize the crystal structure of materials with Cu Ka as source of radiation, k = 1.54056 Å at a range from 10° to 80°. The morphology and element species of the samples were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss Gemini 300) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). Nitrogen adsorption-desorption data were obtained with Micromeritics ASAP2020M analyzer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured with an ESCALAB 250Xi using Mg Kα line as the excitation source. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy were recorded on an infrared spectrometer in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 (FTIR, TENSOR 37). The zeta potential of the adsorbent was measured using a zeta potential meter (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90). After experiment, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter and the residual concentrations of arsenic species were measured by inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES, iCAP 7200).

Adsorption kinetic experiments were investigated in a series of 50 mL conical flasks containing 0.015 g of adsorbent (0.5 g L−1) and 30 mL of As(V) (pH = 3.0 ± 0.2) or As(III) solution (pH = 11 ± 0.2) with an initial concentration of 10 mg L−1 at 25 °C. The mixtures were shaken with the rate of 300 rpm at 25 °C and samples were withdrawn within the given time interval and filtered through 0.22 µm membrane filters for further analysis by ICP-OES. For adsorption isotherm model experiments, the initial As(V) solution (pH = 3.0 ± 0.2) concentration settings range from 10 to 120 mg L−1 and the dosage of adsorbent was 0.015 g. Samples were withdrawn at the equilibrium (40 min) and filtered through 0.22 µm membrane filters for further analysis. The removal efficiency (R%) was calculated by the following equations:
R % =
1 -
C e
/
C 0
× 100 %
In the equation, C0 (mg L−1) and Ce (mg L−1) represent to the initial and equilibrium concentration of As(III) or As(V) in solution, respectively.

The initial solution pH was adjusted to a specific value by 0.1 M NaOH or HCl to investigate the effect of solution pH on As(III) or As(V) adsorption, the initial concentration of As(III) or As(V) is 10 mg L−1, and the dosage of FeCD-NH is 0.5 g L−1. In addition, the effect of anions and heavy metals on As(V) adsorption were investigated in the presence of NaCl, Na2CO3, Na3PO4, Na2SO4, Na2SiO3, NaNO3, NaHCO3, ZnCl2, CuCl2, K2Cr2O7, CdCl2 and Pb(NO3)2 at a concentration of 20 mg L−1. To examine the performance of FeCD-NH in practical applications, experiments were conducted with natural groundwater spiked with As(V), including lake water and well water. The spiked concentration of As(V) was 10 mg L−1. In order to test the reusability of FeCD-NH, the first cycle of regeneration experiment was carried out with 0.015 g adsorbent in 30 mL As(V) solution (10 mg L−1, pH = 3 ± 0.2). After adsorption equilibrium, 0.5 M NaOH was used to elute As(V) adsorbed on the adsorbent, followed by UP water washing to neutral. After drying the adsorbent, it was added into another 30 mL As(V) solution to start the second cycle experiment. Repeat the above experimental steps 5 times.

The geometry optimization of studied systems (denoted by A1, A2, H2AsO4 −, H2AsO3 −) were performed at the B3LYP-D3BJ/6–311 + G(d) level of theory via the Gaussian 16 suite of programs. The complexes formed between A1(A2) and H2AsO4 − (H2AsO3 −) were optimized and the interaction energies were calculated at the same level. The solvent (H2O) effect was included in the calculations using the solvation model based on the density (SMD) model. The vibrational frequencies of the optimized structures were carried out, and the structures were characterized as a local energy minimum on the potential energy surface by verifying that all the vibrational frequencies were real.

FeCD-NH material was synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method (Fig. 1 a), the carbonyl group in maleimide is protonated to form –OH, and then the –OH groups are dehydration and carbonization to produce the MCD. As shown in TEM images (Fig. 1b and c) and SEM-EDS images (Fig. S1), magnetite crystal nanoparticles were successfully encapsulated in carbon dots, and >90 % of these carbon dots consisted of nanoparticles of uniform size less than 7 nm diameter (Fig. 1b inlet). The lattice fringes of FeCD-NH can be seen clearly in (Fig. 1c), which the lattice spacing of 0.246 nm and 0.263 nm is assigned to the (311) crystal surface of Fe3O4 and the (100) crystal plane of graphite carbon, respectively, which confirms the co-existence of Fe3O4 and MCD [27,28]. FeCD-NH2 has a morphological structure similar to that of FeCD-NH, see in Fig. S2.

The structural information about the FeCD-NH and Fe3O4 was obtained by XRD (Fig. 1d). Six diffraction peaks of the Fe3O4 were at around 30.37°, 35.65°, 43.53°, 53.85°, 57.37°, and 63.07° corresponded to the (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) crystal planes, respectively, which matched with JCPDS card No. 25–1402 [29,30]. No obvious difference was observed after combining with the MCD, which indicates that the existence of the carbon matrix has no influence on a phase change of iron oxide. The FTIR spectra of maleimide, MCD and FeCD-NH were measured to examine their structures (Fig. 1e). The characteristic peaks at 3420, 3200–3100, 1400 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of –OH, –NH and -CO, and the Fe-O characteristic peak at 570 cm−1 is attributed to Fe3O4 [30]. Moreover, it is noteworthy that the prepared FeCD-NH shows a C-NH characteristic peak at 1800 cm−1 belonging to the imide group on maleimide, which indicates the presence of –NH functional group on the surface of FeCD-NH [31]. VSM and N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms tests were performed on FeCD-NH, and the detailed analysis are shown in Fig. S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption performance of –NH and –NH2 functional groups for As(V)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 a shows the adsorption performance of the adsorbent for As(V) when equipped with different functional groups. Obviously, consistent with previous reports, the introduction of –NH2 functional group improved the removal efficiency of As(V) by the adsorbent to a certain extent compared with the low efficiency FeCD. However, it is more noteworthy that the adsorption efficiency of As(V) by FeCD-NH with the introduction of –NH functional group exhibited a tremendous enhancement and the adsorption equilibrium was reached within 5 min (removal efficiency: 97.80 %). The adsorption rate constant (Fig. 2b) of FeCD-NH for As(V) calculated by the adsorption kinetic model was 0.045 g mg−1 min−1, which was much higher than that of FeCD-NH2 of 0.027 g mg−1 min−1 (removal efficiency: 30.72 %). Therefore, it is speculated that the –NH functional group is more conducive to the adsorption of As(V) than the –NH2 functional group.

To this end, FeCD-NH was synthesized with different mass ratios of MCD precursors to vary the content of –NH functional groups in the material and to test the removal efficiency and adsorption kinetic rate of As(V). The results are shown in Fig. 2c, and the removal efficiency and adsorption kinetic rate of As(V) gradually increased from 14.26 % and 0.012 g mg−1 min−1 to 97.80 % and 0.045 g mg−1 min−1, respectively, with the increase of MCD ratio. Apparently, the adsorption rate of As(V) was improved due to the increase of –NH functional group in FeCD-NH with high MCD ratios, which provided more binding sites for As(V). This further demonstrates that –NH functional groups occupy an important role in the adsorption of As(V). In addition, FeCD-NH and FeCD-NH2 were also used for the adsorption of As(III), exhibiting similar results, with 69.15 % removal of As(III) by FeCD-NH within 120 min, while FeCD-NH2 was only 26.87 % (Fig. S4a). We also demonstrate the contribution of Fe3O4 to the adsorption of As(III) and As(V), and the results were shown in Fig. S4b that the removal efficiency of Fe3O4 for As(III) and As(V) is only 23.60 % and 8.41 %, which further confirms the importance played by the –NH functional group in the adsorption of arsenic.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of as adsorption on FeCD-NH and FeCD-NH2
Adsorption isotherm model and adsorption kinetics
ce:section.ce:para: The Freundlich and Langmuir isotherm models were clarified to discuss and determine the mechanism involved in FeCD-NH removal of As(V), as listed in Table S1, compared to Freundlich (R2 = 0.9692), the adsorption data can be better fitted by Langmuir model (R2 = 0.9980), which indicates that the monomolecular layer adsorption of As(V) on FeCD-NH [24]. The adsorption kinetics of FeCD-NH and FeCD-NH2 on As(III) and As(V) were investigated and all kinetic curves were fitted using pseudo-first-order (PFO) kinetic and pseudo-senond-order (PSO) kinetic models, which are commonly used for adsorption kinetic simulations [17–19]. The obtained results were shown in Table S2 and Table S3, compared with the PFO (R2 = 0.7846 for FeCD-NH, R2 = 0.9404 for FeCD-NH2) model in Table S2, the higher R2 value indicates that the PSO model (R2 = 0.9963 for FeCD-NH, R2 = 0.9784 for FeCD-NH2) better describes the As(V) adsorption process on FeCD-NH and FeCD-NH2, which means that the adsorption of As(V) is a chemisorption process [18,19]. According to the data in Table S3, the adsorption of As(III) by FeCD-NH and FeCD-NH2 also follows the PSO model, attributed to the higher R2 value (R2 PSO = 0.9666 > R2 PSO = 0.8187 for FeCD-NH, R2 PSO = 0.9137 > R2 PSO = 0.8212 for FeCD-NH2). In addition, an increase in the adsorption rate (k2) of FeCD-NH (0.045 g mg−1 min−1) relative to FeCD-NH2 (0.027 g mg−1 min−1) implies an enhanced chemisorption between As(V) and the imide-equipped FeCD-NH. Moreover, a substantially higher adsorption rate (k2) was observed for FeCD-NH compared to other As(V) adsorbents in the literature (Fig. S5), which could be attributed to the stronger chemisorption between FeCD-NH and As(V) molecules.

To get an in-depth understanding of the adsorption mechanism of As on FeCD-NH and FeCD-NH2, the effects of electrostatic interaction were considered. Therefore, the effect of pH on adsorption of As(V) and As(III) were investigated by varying the pH in the range of 3–9 and 3–11, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the pH change due to As(V) adsorption was investigated by comparing the equilibrium pH of the FeCD-NH-As(V) system (pHe1) and FeCD-NH2-As(V) system (pHe2) with the equilibrium pH of the at the same initial pH (pHsolution). It shows two stages: when pH = 3–8, pHe1 > pHe2 ≈ pHsolution, meaning that at pHe = 3–8, OH– is released or H+ is adsorbed due to the interaction between FeCD-NH and As(V). When pHe > 8, pHe1 ≈ pHe2 ≈ pHsolution, there is no pH change. Similarly, in Fig. S6a, when pH = 3–4, pHe1 ≈ pHe2 ≈ pHsolution, while when pH > 4, pHe1 < pHe2 ≈ pHsolution. While FeCD-NH2 has no obvious fluctuation in pH throughout the process of adsorption of As(V) and As(III), indicating that FeCD-NH and FeCD-NH2 have different adsorption mechanisms for As.

As shown in Fig. 3c, the removal efficiency of As(V) gradually decreases with the increase of pH, which is similar to the results reported in the previous literature [32,33]. Specifically, when pH = 3, >90 % of As(V) is adsorbed, and when pH = 4–9, the adsorption efficiency is reduced from 85.43 % to 65.71 %. According to the dissociation constants of As(V) in aqueous phase are pKa1 = 2.30, pKa2 = 6.80, pKa3 = 11.30 (Fig. 3b), that is, the anionic As(V) species would change from H2AsO4 −, HAsO4 2− and AsO4 3− in the range of pH from 2 to 14 [34]. As can be seen in the zeta potential of FeCD-NH in Fig. 3c, the zero charge (pHPZC) was at 7.7. When pH < 7.7, the positive charge on the surface of FeCD-NH can be attributed to the functional groups and the protonation reaction on the surface of the adsorbent [35]. While, when pH > 7.7, with the gradual decrease of H+ in the solution, more OH– tends to accumulate on the surface of the adsorbent [36]. As a result, the negative charge on the adsorbent surface continued to increase, which interacted with the electrostatic repulsion between the negatively charged HAsO4 2− and AsO4 3−, thus reducing the As(V) removal efficiency [33]. As shown in Fig. S6b, when the pH value ranges from 2 to 14, the species of As(III) will change from H3AsO3 to H2AsO3 − and HAsO3 2−. Therefore, the adsorption of FeCD-NH on As(III) can also be attributed to electrostatic attraction.

However, the rules of electrostatic attraction didn’t seem to apply to the adsorption of As by FeCD-NH2. As shown in Fig. 3d and Fig. S6d, the zeta potential of FeCD-NH2 gradually decreased in pH = 3–9, while the adsorption efficiency of As(V) and As(III) did not change with the change of pH, and remained at ∼17 % and ∼35 %. These results showed that electrostatic attraction is not the dominant force for FeCD-NH2 to adsorb As(V) and As(III).

Apparently, FeCD-NH and FeCD-NH2 each possessed different morphological H donors, –NH and –NH2, respectively, which means that there may be differences in the form of hydrogen bond with As(V). Since the formation of hydrogen bonds between amino groups and As(V) is well documented, differences in hydrogen bond formation were identified by XPS and FTIR. The FTIR spectrum before and after As(V) adsorption by FeCD-NH is shown in Fig. 4a. It can be clearly observed that the absence of the characteristic peak attributed to the imide functional group (C-NH, 1800 cm−1) in FeCD-NH after the adsorption of As(V) suggests that the imide functional group may be involved in the formation of hydrogen bonds with As(V). In addition, the characteristic peak attributed to the As-O stretching mode, which appears in the FT-IR spectrum of FeCD-NH after As(V) adsorption (As-O, 860 cm−1), definitely confirming the successful adsorption of As(V) [37]. In Fig. 4b, the appearance of the As-O peak at 864 cm−1 after As(V) adsorption by FeCD-NH2 was also observed, but the association of –NH2 with As(V) could not be accurately concluded, and therefore, a more in-depth investigation was needed.

In order to effectively distinguish the hydrogen bonds formed between FeCD-NH and FeCD-NH2 with As(V), XPS was used to analyze the difference of functional groups before and after adsorption. As shown in Fig. 4c, the N 1 s high-resolution XPS spectrum of FeCD-NH shows two deconvolution peaks at 399.4 and 400.3 eV, which are assigned to C-NH-C and N···HO species, respectively, suggesting that FeCD-NH can form two different hydrogen bonds, i.e. (1) as an H donor, forming NH···O hydrogen bonds, and (2) as an H acceptor, forming N···HO hydrogen bonds [27]. Compared with the sample before As(V) adsorption, the intensity of C-NH-C decreased significantly from 88.07 % to 67.05 %, while the content of N···HO increased from 11.93 % to 32.95 %. Consequently, it is reasonable to conclude that N···HO hydrogen bond formation plays a vital and positive role in the adsorption process of As(V). On the other hand, the –NH2 functional group can also act as both H acceptor and donor to produce hydrogen bonds of two configurations. However, it can be observed from Fig. 4d that the content of –NH2 (399.8 eV) decreases (from 79.77 to 74.61 %) after the adsorption of As(V) by FeCD-NH2, indicating that hydrogen bond of NH···O configuration is involved in the adsorption of As(V). While, the change of N···HO content was negligible (from 15.07 to 14.61 %), indicating that N···HO configuration hydrogen bond did not contribute to the adsorption of As(V). After As(III) adsorption by FeCD-NH and FeCD-NH2, the N 1 s high-resolution XPS spectrum analysis were displayed in Fig. S7. The intensity of C-NH-C decreased to 54.65 % while the content of N···HO increased to 45.35 % and also proved the positive effect of N···HO on As(III) adsorption. Meanwhile, only the –NH2 content of FeCD-NH2 decreased significantly after As(III) adsorption, while N···HO content did not change significantly, it can therefore be concluded that NH···O hydrogen bond dominated by –NH2 functional groups made the major contribution during the adsorption of As(III).

The C 1 s high-resolution XPS spectrum of FeCD-NH before and after As adsorption are dominated by three main peaks in Fig. 4 e and Fig. S7c, which are respectively attributed to CC (285.3 eV), CO (286.4 eV) and NH-CO (288.8 eV). Due to the strong electron-withdrawing of the two adjacent carbonyl groups in the imide group, the chemical polarity of the NH bond increases, resulting in the easy dissociation of H of the –NH group as a proton (H+), so that –NH can be better acted as a H donor, which is consistent with the literature report [38,39]. In other words, after –NH is made to contribute protons as H donor, the isolated N atom can better act as H acceptor, thus forming N···HO hydrogen bond with As. After the adsorption of As(V), the CC peak shifts by 0.5 eV to 284.8 eV, the NH-CO peak shifts by 0.4 eV to 284.4 eV with a broadening of the peak shape. Similarly, after adsorption of As(III), the CC peak shifts to 285.1 eV, and the NH-CO peak shifts to 288.6 eV. These peak shift can be explained by the increase of electron density on carbonyl carbon caused by As adsorption [39]. On the contrary, there are only two peaks in the C 1 s spectrum of FeCD-NH2 (Fig. 4f and Fig. S7d), which belong to CC and CO, and no NH-CO peak is observed, which indicates that the externally introduced –NH2 did not form a proximal structure with CO, without the electron-withdrawing effect of the adjacent carbonyl group, the H in –NH2 does not dissociate and prefers to act as a proton donor to form NH···O between As.

In order to distinguish the effects of –NH and –NH2 functional groups on As(III/V), we further studied the possible interaction scenarios of FeCD-NH and FeCD-NH2 with As(III/V) at the molecular level through DFT calculation. The optimal configuration of FeCD-NH and H2AsO4 − is shown in Fig. 5 a. The –NH group on FeCD-NH provides additional adsorption sites for As(V) through two hydrogen bonding forms, NH···O and N···HO. The adsorption energy generated by the synergistic effect of two hydrogen bonds is −63.06 kJ mol−1, which further improves the adsorption affinity. While –NH2 formed in FeCD-NH2 and As(V) has a weaker hydrogen bonding adsorption energy of −35.32 kJ mol−1 for NH···O. Similarly, the synergistic interaction of the two hydrogen bonds by –NH produced a larger adsorption energy (Fig. S8a, −42.92 kJ mol−1) for As(III) compared to the single hydrogen bonded –NH2 (Fig. S8b, –33.15 kJ mol−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of As(V) adsorption on FeCD-NH and FeCD-NH2
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above results, As(V) is taken as an example to reveal the difference in adsorption mechanism between FeCD-NH2 and FeCD-NH (Fig. 6 ). First of all, in the process of As(V) adsorption by FeCD-NH2, there was only NH···O hydrogen bond made a contribution, and unlike the traditional conclusion that electrostatic attraction is inapplicable to describe the adsorption of As(V) by FeCD-NH2. While in the adsorption of As(V) by FeCD-NH, electrostatic attraction plays an active role in the adsorption process, and FeCD-NH has a higher isoelectric point than FeCD-NH2 (Fig. 3c and d), which means that FeCD-NH has a positive charge even at high pH. Therefore, FeCD-NH exhibited excellent adsorption performance on negatively charged arsenic contaminants through electrostatic interactions. In addition, hydrogen bonding is one of the important factors for the efficient adsorption of As(V) by FeCD-NH, –NH acts as a proton donor and forms a NH···O hydrogen bond with As(V). Meanwhile, due to its lone pair of electrons, the N atom tends to exhibit a strong affinity for protons [40], N atoms can also act as H acceptors, thus forming N···HO hydrogen bonds with –OH on As(V), which based on the XPS results, contribute significantly to the adsorption of As(V). Thus, the adsorption energy of FeCD-NH with As(V) is higher than that of FeCD-NH2 under the synergistic effect of hydrogen bonds of dual-conformations.

In environmental media, there are usually complex anions and cations that interfere with the adsorption performance of the adsorbent for the target contaminant and PO4 3− has the greatest effect on the adsorption of As(V) due to structural similarities [35,41]. However, as shown in Fig. S9a, there was no significant influence on the removal efficiency of As(V) by FeCD-NH in the low concentrations coexisting system (0.1 M). When the ionic strength increased from 1 to 10 M, the removal efficiency of As(V) decreased slightly, still remained above 80 %, indicating that FeCD-NH showed excellent selectivity, which may be the result of the unique adsorption mechanism. On the other hand, as shown in Fig. S9b, in the As(V)-coexisting heavy metal system, FeCD-NH also exhibits excellent selectivity and has almost no effect on the removal efficiency of As(V), indicating that FeCD-NH has good anti-interference properties and can effectively resist the effect of coexisting ions.

In practical applications, the regeneration and reusability of the adsorbent are considered to be the most important determining parameters for examining the stability and cost of the adsorbent [35]. Therefore, the reusability of FeCD-NH is evaluated by performing five consecutive recyclings and 0.5 M NaOH was used as an eluent. As shown in Fig. 7 a, after five cycles of adsorption-desorption process, the removal efficiency of As(V) by FeCD-NH was slightly decreased from 98.30 % to 92.50 %, and the Fe leaching concentration was below the maximum contamination for groundwater (0.3 ppm) set by quality standard for ground water. Meanwhile, SEM, XRD and XPS results (Fig. S10) showed that the surface morphology, chemical composition and crystal structure of FeCD-NH have not changed significantly after several cycles. The results proved that FeCD-NH can be regenerated and reused for many times, which confirms the practical value and application of FeCD-NH as As adsorbent.

On the other hand, to test whether FeCD-NH could effectively remove As(V) for use in natural water, experiments were performed using As(V)-containing well water and lake water as simulated wastewater, respectively. The experimental results show in Fig. 7b that under the condition of pristine pH (pHwell = 7.49, pHlake = 7.67) and the dosage of FeCD-NH is 1.5 g L−1, FeCD-NH could remove 97.50 % and 92.30 % As(V) in well water and lake water, respectively, within 5 min. After adsorption equilibrium, As(V) is completely removed to satisfy both the WHO drinking water standards and the Chinese surface water standards. This demonstrated that FeCD-NH can maintain the outstanding adsorption performance even in complex environment which has a promising prospect in practical environment remediation.

In this work, we have explored in detail the differences in the adsorption mechanism of As(III/V) on two different amino-liked functional groups using carbon dots as carriers. The results suggest that (1) FeCD-NH with imide groups can adsorb As contaminants by electrostatic interaction; (2) the imide groups can act as both proton donors and proton acceptors to form hydrogen bonds of different conformations with As(III/V), which enhances the adsorption of As(III/V) under the synergistic effect of dual-configuration hydrogen bonds. Therefore, it is expected to be used for the removal of other anionic contaminants with strong H-donor groups by appropriate modification of imide groups, which needs further exploration. Moreover, FeCD-NH showed an ultrafast adsorption rate for As(V) contaminant and exhibited excellent reusability and practicality. Consequently, this study provides an important theoretical and experimental basis for the practical application of amino-functionalization of adsorbent to improve arsenic pollution remediation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.141540.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Zhou, Y. G. Liu, S. B. Liu, H. Y. Liu, G. M. Zeng, X. F. Tan, C. P. Yang, Y. Ding, Z. L. Yan, X. X. Cai Sorption performance and mechanisms of arsenic(V) removal by magnetic gelatin-modified biochar. Chem. Eng. J. 314(2016), 223-231, 10.1016/j.cej.2016.12.113
